speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 3. You were saying that you managed to escape from Poland. Could I ask before you talk about that, did you personally experience the growing anti-Semitism in Poland after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, the worst part was with my friend. That was very, very sad. I still get upset when I think about it. But then it falls in place, you see. Yes, anti-Semitism was very rampant in Poland. Bandits were running around. Shortly before we left Poland, there was a [INAUDIBLE] pogrom, which everybody knew-- every Jew in Poland knew about it-- and this is when they released-- they-- they allowed us to escape to the borders. They were closing their eyes for Jews to escape. Although it was already under the communist regime, they let us go because there was no future for Jews in Poland, really. I mean, they were just being killed and the partisans that came out of the forest, by-- by various people that were killing Jews. One of the jeweler who was in the house was killed by bandits. Two bandits brought him to the house because they wanted his money. They [INAUDIBLE] Lodz shortly before we left, I still remember. And there was a big commotion, lots of noise, shooting. We all ran in, they ran out. They took what they could, the bandits. We ran in. And that's when I was coming and the fellow was just sliding there and he had his brains blown out on the wall and it just splattered all over the place. And when my mother saw that, she grabbed me and pulled me out and tried to hit me not to watch, not to watch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, this is-- this is part of living. I mean-- I've seen-- I've seen things [INAUDIBLE] for years. I mean, you know. Who gets impressed by someone's brain being blown out? It's just another dead person. You see one, you see them all. You see so many of them. Anyhow, so it wasn't a very-- very pleasant. And it was actually documented about the jeweler being-- being killed. He was one of those people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there were many instances. This is just one of the many instances in Poland. This is the way we were treated after the war. Once we escaped to-- to the border, got caught between-- between Poland and Czechoslovakia-- Poland and Czechoslovakia, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We traveled-- we traveled by-- first of all, by train up to a certain point. It was all arranged. It was money business. You buy [INAUDIBLE] someone pays. It was American money coming in, getting us out, smuggling us. It was a whole game. The whole thing was a game in those days. And so we got up to the Czechoslovakian border, then we're told to walk across. The guard took his money. Walk across and you'll be safe. And someone flashing the lights. The ones who were flashing lights were guards and they caught us deliberately and they put us in jail and then we were smuggled out and taken to Germany. But we had to be bought out again. We spent about six or seven days in jail. It's the only time that I actually finished up in jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've done a lot of dishonest things in my life, but I've never been in jail except for doing nothing. That's-- that's a bit hard, isn't it? OK. I'm-- I'm only actually joking, but it is on the this tape. But yes, they put us in jail. Yes, sure. Now, we were taken to Stuttgart-- And who were the people that put you in jail? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they caught us, the-- what do you call it-- guards on the Czech-German border. It was a combination of the borders that were taken and checked here and checked. But it was all money, you see. It was a game. They could put us in. It wasn't because we were criminal. We crossed the borders illegally, but you pay. They come every day, Americans come with trucks, military men carrying money in suitcases. Come your turn and you get out. If you're important, you get out quickly. We weren't all that important, so we stayed there for about a week in Germany. It was OK. Plenty of food, no problem. That was after the war, it was all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went to Stuttgart. Why went to Stuttgart? Because a lot of people from Radom went to Stuttgart, apparently. And we were reunited with a lot of children from Radom. Some I knew, most I didn't. And altogether, there were 27 children that supposedly have survived the war in the orphanage up in Stuttgart from Radom. 27 children out of a population of Radom and all its surrounding, which was 87,000, which would have been about 30,000 children, 27 survived. So it's one child in 1,000, one child in 1,200.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is why I was saying that you grow up very quickly, you see. The children that survived, of course, a lot of luck has to be, as well. Many of these children come from Russia, so only about half or a quarter of those children survive in actual Poland itself. So you get about six children in the 27,000 that survive in Poland. They are lucky and other things. You grow up quickly and sort of you get away with just-- you duck the bullets and keep away from the dogs and get killed and you survive. That's a simple recipe, but, you know, I wouldn't advise it to any other child to proceed. I mean, I don't find it all that beneficial. I mean, it was exciting, but I could give that a miss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the children talk about the experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. None of us talked much about that. We just wanted to get on with-- with life. A most unusual thing happened, as well. Life wasn't all that boring for me. When we first got up to that camp, Stuttgart, in the displaced people's camp, where I for the first time started going to school, I was learning Hebrew because many of these children went to Palestine, were going to Palestine. In those days, it was Palestine. Orphans, most of them were orphans. Some had families, but many were orphans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was organizing the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was those places-- well, we were with family. Those-- some had family, some had orphanages were organized by Joint, which is an American organization, Jewish American organization-- funding. And they were paying for those things. And an unusual thing happened. Because when I first come in with the children on the very first day that we went to school, we were given uniforms without any insignia. They were just brown uniforms. And when we went to school, people started throwing things at us. We couldn't work out what it was. America was so naive. We were in the American zone in Stuttgart. And they weren't aware of the fact that those uniforms they gave us were old uniforms of Hitler Youth. And I thought it was funny I'd finish up with a uniform of Hitler. Life can be funny. So we-- we got out of the uniform very quickly and we just wore whatever clothes come from America. I don't remember. I finished up with a fur coat. I finished up with a fur coat, a girl's fur coat, I think. I'm not sure what it was. But I had a fur coat at one stage, one of the winters in Germany. We went to school, we learned Hebrew, we learned Yiddish, and we-- we writed and writed. And finally, my uncle from Paris, my mother's brother, found out that we're in-- in the displacement camp, we're spending nine months to one year. All of-- last of '46, beginning of '47. He got us out, also some money-- he got us set up with the money, got us out, took us to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With me all the time. He took us to Paris. We went by train from Stuttgart to Paris. We were met there by my family-- my uncle; his wife; my cousin same age as me, girl; and another little girl who was only about three or four years old, could barely walk. We were taken in, given a good wash, and we had the whole family getting together. There was another uncle who survived. In France, most of my family survived. In Poland, only my mother and I from Poland-- other than Uncle [INAUDIBLE] in Russia. But in France, most of my-- my father's brother survived, his wife perished, his three daughters survived, my mother's brother survived who was also a doctor, studied together with my father. My father helped him out through their studies. He apparently wasn't quite as bright. Everyone seems to tell me that my father was very bright so he make sure that I wouldn't study medicine so I wouldn't compete with him. I choose-- I choose engineering instead when I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the brother my father helped. That's all relevant to the next part. Were they also in camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My-- my uncle was a military doctor who escaped with-- the Normandy. Came back with the Normandy landing with [INAUDIBLE] and came back. But he-- he escaped during the French-- when France was invaded and they-- to Calais. They went across. He was wounded, he was decorated, he came back, he was made a hero and so he was given a surgery and he was a really honored person. Another uncle escaped and lived in the Lyon, somewhere free France on the other side of France. The children and wife were in-- in hiding in-- in-- in-- in Paris, hidden by collaborators, French-- I mean French sympathizers of-- of-- of [INAUDIBLE]. They all survived. Only one died. Only one of them-- my auntie died, one of the aunties from the Rozen side of the family, my father's sister-in-law, died and another-- another cousin with another part-- mother, father, and cousin my age were all taken to Auschwitz from France.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually, three-- only four members of the family died in-- in France and 23 died in-- in-- out of 25 died in Poland. So we had a bad run in Poland. So that big dinner, that was-- that was-- that was a big dinner. And I think you better get ready for a-- for a hard landing. Things got worse. I told you I'd run out of luck after the war. Things were OK during the war, to some extent-- but all that good, but to some extent. But I ran out of luck in Paris. That was bad news, again

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a family dinner, everybody was there. And why I run out of luck-- luck-- there-- most of them spoke in French, I couldn't understand. My uncle spoke to me in Polish. His wife was Russian so she couldn't speak Polish. So everybody talked and so on. And everybody was eating and eating and eating and talking and this and excitement. It as about 12 people or so. And where I run out of luck is because I was put close to the meat. There was a roast. I hadn't seen a roast for a long time. And that's the bad news. What I did with the roast is what anybody would do as smart as me. I was a smart guy then. I was 10 years old and I was professional. I knocked off half of the roast and put it inside my shirt so I knew the next day, we were going to eat this well. And that's when the roof came down. My white Russian auntie got me by the ear, pulled my ear. They started working me, hitting me. After they hit and whacked me for five minutes, I explained to my uncle in Polish to tell them I've spent two years with partisans. If she thinks I'm going to cry, she said her hand will be much sorer than it is now before I start crying, so don't waste your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she hit harder and the results were the same. Partisans don't cry. So we-- but she got the better of me, God bless her. She's been dead for quite some time. She got the better of me. My auntie put me in an orphanage for four and a half years because of my [INAUDIBLE]. And that's a hard [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Things got tough. If you see the cut here across my nose, that was a razor from the orphanage because we'd-- we'd settle arguments with knives and razors. So he got me with a knife-- with a razor. He was a much taller boy than me. And while he was trying to get the razor out, it got stuck. He put it in pretty deep. I got my knife out and did my first bit of surgery. I cut in a circle and was taken to hospital. I don't know what happened to him. I-- came back. That's the life that you live in-- scars here, that was another knife. Cuts across there. That was a tough life for four and a half years. If-- if the war was hard, four and a half years in French orphanages with delinquents, that was bad news. Shit, that was bad news. That was a hard landing. So you win some, you lose some. If I hadn't sat so close to the roast, I probably would have had a different life altogether. But that was hard in France, that was hard. My uncle and auntie, who came from Poland to Russia to Germany from France to Australia found out about our plight and they got-- they got my mother and I out of the France and we finished up in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me what was your mother's reaction when the auntie--

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't do anything. She couldn't speak French, she couldn't work. It was the best thing for me. But I was a-- I was a typical case for a-- for the orphanage. I was wild, I didn't have any type of manners. I had never used a knife or a fork for six years or whatever. I was bored. I was bored. And they never gave me a chance. One night. I guess [INAUDIBLE] a little lot of meat out of it. I [INAUDIBLE]. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was tough. the main reason given to me afterwards-- the reason given to me afterwards was well, everything becomes normal is because they had two daughters and doctors' daughters and so on with a savage like me in the family, what chance? There was no chance for me. I mean, I could-- I had to be with the other savages, all the kids that came out of camps and so on. The games we used to play, we used to lie on railway slippers ad wait till the train comes and the bravest one was the one that would roll off the last.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some took too long. I always rolled off. They were the games we used to play. They do similar things now with surfing on trains, but we used to do the rails. I always rolled off. Sometimes I would just about feel it. And I could-- as the train gets close to the locomotive, you can just about feel the-- the vibration [INAUDIBLE] already. But you know exactly when to roll off. Those are the sort of games you play in orphanages. And when you don't knife yourself. It was bad news, [INAUDIBLE] bad news. Come to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with your mother while you were in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I used to go to Paris. Once a fortnight, I used to go to Paris. And after a while when I straightened up a little bit and my manners improved, I was impeccable. I was-- I was tuned in. I went to school in an orphanage for years, right through high school. I became a chess-- a junior chess champion at the French high school there. And we used to fight in the orphanages with knives because the boys in the orphanage had uniforms, gray uniforms, and we're different to all-- you know, Jewish all Jewish. And oddly enough, one of the famous kids in the orphanage-- not in ours, but in the one up the hill-- who were the [INAUDIBLE] boys, who came out of Buchenwald, was Elie Weasel was in the same group of orphanages I was. [INAUDIBLE]. And he is most famous kids from the orphanage. Didn't do him much harm, either. [INAUDIBLE] was there, too. I don't know if you heard about him. He was a famous lawyer, international lawyer. But well, we got one Nobel prize winner out of-- out of the orphanages so it couldn't have been that bad. We got a grand master in bridge, as well. He's talking to you at the moment. But we'll come to that later. So things weren't all that bad in the orphanage. And yes, I used to come once a fortnight to Paris. And my cousin used to take me to the pictures. I was never allowed to handle the money because I was not to be trusted. So my cousin used to buy me my ice cream and so on and so on. And one day, I still remember today, which is another-- another bad day for me, that was already a year or two after I was in the orphanage. My uncle gave me 200 francs, which is equivalent to two-- two pictures' ticket, about $1 for-- on today's cost.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me 200 francs. It was after the war, 200 francs, not today's. And-- and I was looking at it because I had never had any money of my own. So I was looking at it, that short auntie-- she was six foot wide and five foot tall-- she ran across the passage. She must have broken the-- the-- the 20 meters world record. She ran across the passage so quickly, grabbed the money out of my hand as I was looking at it, turned around to my uncle. He says, why do he give you money? Nobody else got money in the orphanage. Why should you be any different?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't understand. But the penny dropped. Did she cover those 10 meters fast. You've never seen a-- a fat lady that big running that quickly. Shit, she got the money so quickly-- unbelievable. Oh, pardon me. I use bad language sometimes. Still my bad manners. I had-- I had-- I just had bad luck there in France. Things weren't right. Just things were rotten in France. The war was bad, but at least there was reason for it. But after the war, it was bad for no reason. An orphanage, that was bad news.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep your hope there at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to school, played basketball, played chess. I was doing all right. There was no problem, I was just growing up. We had lots of games. I remember I had a friend who was so much smarter than me, Michel. I don't know what happened to him. I said there were two things would happen to him. He'll either get his-- three things. He'll either get his neck cut by somebody, he'll become the prime minister of France, or he will spend the rest of his days in jail. I don't know which three of the things happened. He didn't become the prime minister of France and I don't know what happened to Michel. I still remember a big boy-- bigger boy, taller than I. Similar [INAUDIBLE], but he was so much smarter than me. He also was a survivor from somewhere, but he could speak French so he was a French orphan that survived through the similar circumstance to what I did. But he was so much smarter. And once, I got the-- I got my best marks on the [INAUDIBLE] I ever got. We used to go to Paris and we used to play games because we were poor and we didn't have any money when we went to the family. So we used to do all sorts of things. We could get in-- we'd get into theaters, we'd get into-- I heard Edith Piaf sing in 1949 in the Palais [? DeVere ?] for nothing. We used to go-- we used to go without-- with another family. We pretended that we were part of the other children and when other children sneak through, we used to sneak through. He taught me how to get in, you now. Then we'd come in before they had time, we'd sneak through. But not only that, but we were so smart-- Michel was so smart. We used to go up to the [FRENCH], which is the very top, the cheapest place, so we know that up there there's always empty seats because people don't go that far. That's about the fifth floor. It's called the [FRENCH] in French. It's the [INAUDIBLE] or whatever you call it in English, I think. And we would watch after the first act to see whether there were some empty seats and we would count them-- we were so smart. We'd count them. We'd end up sometimes in the best [INAUDIBLE]. Sometimes they would throw us out and sometimes we'd get away with it. And we would sit next to other children with empty seats and we'd get away with it like we're part of the family and we sort of-- and I remember one of the tricks that we used to have, one of his tricks, but I've learned my lesson.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to duck quickly. I-- I learn quickly, I'm a good learner. I'm not always clever, but a good leaner. We used to go to Paris and we used to follow these good-looking mademoiselles that walked with a-- they used to walk with a wiggle in France, the ladies. Now it's not fashionable, but it was-- do you know how they used to wiggle in the '40s? If you've seen in some of the films? Yeah, you have? [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they wiggled like that. And one day, those wiggling ladies, we used to follow them-- Michel and I used to follow them. So he-- he would place me in front of him and I would just walk. And we'd walk along the Champs Elysee in Paris, walking around behind these elegant ladies and pretending to be wiggling like that. And people would laugh. And one of them turned around and gave me such a whack. It was all his fault, you see. So next time we'd wiggle around and when they turned around, I stood back. So that was that-- and then run away. And we used to play another sort of stupid game. Another thing-- another one of his favorite games was it worked-- always worked, every time it worked when we're in Paris. We used to also walk around somewhere and create a commotion and pretend that we're pointing at-- look, look, look, look and we're screaming and so on. And everybody-- everybody would stop. We'd get up to 100 people looking up and they'd be pointing at it and looking up. They didn't know what they looking-- we were pointing at a building, like pretending that someone is jumping out of the window. And we were saying, yes, they're going to jump out. Look, look, look, he's gone inside. He's jumping-- jumping out. And everybody would look around because we didn't have any toys in those days. So we-- we used-- he was smarter than I was. They were all his tricks. I've learned all those tricks from him. And you point up and you know you'd have 100 people pointing and they all see see people jumping out of windows. And it was all just-- we just-- just took the Mickey out of them. We used to have some regular friends, too, we used see them at [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] are people who don't have any home where homeless people drink methylated spirit or whatever and they-- in certain places. And they were very nice. They were nice. We used to talk to them because most people didn't want to talk to us, kids from orphanages, you know, bad news. I mean, you might catch something if you talk to us. But they didn't mind, you know. So they felt at home and we felt at home. So life went on in Paris like that until we came to Australia. We arrived in Australia in 1951, March the 14th, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you arrange the arrival to Australia? How did you do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle guaranteed-- my uncle and auntie from Russia that my father married came here. They had a-- they bought a house, half a house, in South Caulfield, 23 Steele Street, South Caulfield Brought us here and we came by boat that lasted a long time, for weeks, weeks, weeks. Through the Suez Canal weren't allowed to leave, Jewish people weren't allowed to leave in those days. And we came and I still remember like today another one of those episodes. We got up to my-- my uncle's place. He had a son-- I was then 14, 15, he had a son of about 10, 11 that was born during the war and another little one that was born in Paris on the way through that was only about five or six. And auntie and uncle and my mother, we were all sitting in the kitchen and in those days, they had bottles of milk, glass bottles of milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my cousin pours a bottle of milk and as soon as he started pouring it, I just said, I've learned my lesson. So I sat on my hands. I sat on my hands, I didn't take any food. I'm a quick learner. I didn't touch anything, I didn't pinch anything. Nothing in my shirt, nothing at all. I [INAUDIBLE] and says, why don't you eat? I says, what can I have? I was very well brought up. After four and a half years in the orphanage, just you get struck and that. No question about it, you survive. If you don't, well, the manners go with you. So what would you like to eat? And I said, what-- what can I eat? Anything. Can I have some milk? Yeah. How much milk can I have?

speaker: interviewee
text: As much as you want. And I still remember pouring. I poured and I poured and I poured and I poured and poured until the glass got over. And then I licked all that underneath them and then I-- that's my-- my first full glass of milk I had at the age of 15. So life's been good ever since. Well, you have your ups and down, but compared to the past, it's a good country here. I like it. I've had a good run here, I can't complain. I went to Melbourne High, then I went to RMIT in those days, now it's called something else, University of Technology. Sounds better that way. Studied engineering, went into business at the age of 20 because I decided to make money and I thought studying wasn't for me. I didn't want to become an engineer after two years. I passed, no problem. I won the 19-- in 1953, I won the Victorian Junior School Boys-- Victorian Junior Chess Championships. I was top boy from Melbourne High. That was my first trophy. I formed a club in 1956, a secular chess club, which became one of the leading clubs in Australia for many, many years. I was its founder and president at the ripe age of 20. And from then on, I got married. My first marriage didn't work too well. We had a son. I won the custody of my son. I decided being a winner is better than being a loser, so I contested the custody and my wife got too smart and lost the custody because she didn't think she could lose. In those days, mothers were given custody usually, but this time I got lucky. [INAUDIBLE] some-- some-- some back some [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I retained custody of my son that was looked after by my mother at the age of seven months, he was when that happened. And we lived with Mum for a number of years until about 25 years ago I remarried to my current wife, Michelle, that had two children, also a son the same age as Peter, my son, Peter; and a son, Mark. We also had a daughter that was two years older. Unfortunately, our daughter died when she was 19, some blood disorder she had, a very talented girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: But life was cut short. The boys went on to Brighton Grammar, Melbourne University. Mark finished chemical engineering, got a master in chemical engineering-- that's my wife's son and my son-- and lives now in Adelaide. He lectures and works for the Wine Institute in South Australia in Adelaide, lives on a farm, as well, has a lady friend, lots of sheep and cats and dogs and horses and everything else. Peter finished law, honors degree master's in law. He is now a consultant working for himself. He just married recently and and his wife is also a practicing lawyer, associated in one of the large firms. He's a consultant and he is lecturing at Melbourne University in health society occupation. He is not a permanent lecturer, he is what is known as a visiting lecturer. He's a research fellow at Melbourne University.

speaker: interviewer
text: He has just recently written a book on the health and safety occupation with Professor [INAUDIBLE], so Rozen and [INAUDIBLE]. It's been in the news quite often. And he's-- he's had a much better run than I, I think. And so they live in Melbourne and what can I say about myself? I've started playing bridge about 30 years ago. I've had a good run.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've become eight years ago a grand master in bridge. In 1992, I was-- represented Australia in the world championship in France and in the Olympics.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you to come back to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was OK this time. This time was OK. This time was OK. It's different. Well, I only met my cousins. My auntie and uncle perished. Just my cousins, so it was nice. It was nice. It was my first return. I was very lucky, actually, that I had-- that I was able to make a stipend because I like to make-- I tend to be a vindictive sort of a person. I don't think I'm a real nice person. I mean, my wife tells me I'm not. She doesn't know why she's married to me for so long. It's just that I think fascinate her, she said. I fascinated her when I was young and now she got used to me like old slippers. I think that was the term that she uses. But she was fascinated by it, by the mixture of sort of-- of good and bad and my moods and my pretense and my masquerading, which I have always had. I think it goes back to the day I was a little girl, when I was masquerading, that I can adapt.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel very comfortable with everybody and I try to make everyone feel comfortable with me. But who is the real me? Because my stratum of friends really is from very high to very low on a scale of whatever you want to name it. So I feel quite comfortable with everybody and so I don't really know who I am. But that doesn't matter. I know I'm here. But the point I'm trying to make is that in-- when I was in France, I had an opportunity to make a statement.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when I was a member of the Australian Bridge Team, we had to play in Hamburg. And fortunately, we have three pairs that go together and only two pairs played at a time. And I said before I left that I will not play in Germany. I will never-- I will never put my foot on German soil. I will never-- I will never put my foot on Polish soil. So they had to arrange-- they had to-- first of all, one of the matches was played in London and the other one I just didn't play in Germany. I didn't play in Hamburg. They played without me. But in the Olympics was OK, was OK. Yes, that was in the world champs. That was good, I liked that. Again, my-- my-- my Australian tie to my brother-in-law, who lives in Switzerland. He was very happy to wear my Olympic tie. It's nice. It's a shame I lost it. But I thought, I'll get another one but I didn't. There you are. I didn't get a chance to get into the national team again. This-- this year I was lucky again in bridge. We won the Australian championships just recently in Melbourne held. Australian team-- I was a member of the team that won the Australian championship so I still win a few. I'm now going to Sydney in about four-- beginning of November to play in the Grand National team, so I'm representing Victoria again. So I've had a good run, you know sort of in that respect. Business wise, I can't complain, I make a living. And we've got a holiday house on the peninsula, which we go on weekends. And I-- I-- I am a volunteer at the holocaust center. I spend a great deal of time there, two or three days a week. I'm a president of the Victorian Bridge Association that I've been for eight years and I do a lot of volunteer work there, as well. I work at the National Council of Jewish Women, where my wife has been recently elected as president of the National Council of Jewish Women. And that's-- we are very happy with that. She she's very busy that way. And life is a game and I'm talking to you today. That's-- that's also nice. It's nice to be here. But I'd like to dedicate my talk, and I always do, to the one and a half million children that perished because none of them could talk. I also indicated-- dedicate my talk to the children that survive but are not able to talk or they're too traumatized to talk or they don't have the opportunity. I have all those opportunities. I qualified in the [INAUDIBLE] criteria. I can talk, you gave me the opportunity. Thank you very much and it's been a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
